text: Ah, another day, another chance to witness the horrific spectacle of Disney proddding and kicking the bloated corpse of Star Wars in the desperate hope that it'll let out a burst of flatulent gas which they can pretend is a sign of life. In this case, our latest desperate prod of the corpse is the acolyte. Possibly the most unanticipated and unwanted Lucasfilm project since Willow, [Laughter] a spin-off from the disastrous High Republic books that nobody read,

starring a bunch of characters nobody cares about, played by actors that nobody's heard of, and helmed by a woman that nobody seems to like. Really, what can I say about The Acolyte that would make you want to watch it? as this is, I would say, arguably the gayest Star Wars margin. Anyway, now that I've seen the first two episodes, I'm at least in some position to assess the show's merits. And believe me, this shouldn't take long. The first episode opens with

strong female antagonists challenging strong female Jedi to a fight in a bar. >> Attack me with all your strength. And I must admit, it was nice to see Carrie Moss back in action. And ah, never mind. You know, it's interesting that characters can survive lightsaber injuries like this, but a small throwing knife to the chest is enough to take them down and end their lives. Shows how much I know about human anatomy, I guess. Flash over to the other side of the galaxy, and we're

introduced to strong female character number one. Strong female character number one is a pluckucky young woman with a knack for technology who's also strong with the force. Hm, where have I seen this before? Anyway, she works as a mechanic or sorry, mechnic. Are you serious? On the outer hulls of space freighters doing the usual stuff like repairing power couplings and putting out fires. Sure. Yeah. Okay. I guess fire in the acolyte doesn't need oxygen like in other shows because it no longer

has the patriarchy holding it back. You go, girl. Fire. Things are going great for her until she gets arrested by dumb male character and strong female character number two for the murder of strong female Jedi. See, it turns out that strong female character number one and strong female antagonist are actually twin sisters. And so naturally, people mix them up after the murder of strong female Jedi. And what is all of this based on, you might ask? Why, the eyewitness testimony of the bartender

where strong female Jedi got killed. Not DNA samples, which actually would have made more sense since they're twins. Not hologram recordings or security footage. Just a guy who saw a young woman kill strong female Jedi. Now, you probably don't need me to tell you that I witness testimonies can be notoriously inaccurate, especially in highly stressful situations where our brains release all kinds of chemicals and stimulants to try to keep us alive. chemicals which often collide our

judgment and blur our memory, making us not particularly reliable when it comes to accurate recollection of people and events. I mean, given that he's an alien, I'd have to assume as well that his description of the suspect would probably have boiled down to a young black human female with her hair in braids. Now, in a galaxy composed of thousands of colonized planets, there'd have to be literally billions of people who fit that description. Yet, apparently, this was enough to bring

them straight to strong female character number one. Like how? Also, you might assume that being on the other side of the galaxy at the time of the murder with multiple witnesses to prove you were there and no means of traveling to and from the site of the killing would be a pretty strong alibi, but not according to dumb male character at least. Oh yeah, and in case you were wondering what his role in the story is, it's to be consistently wrong about everything to make the various strong

female characters look better by comparison. Anyway, they decide to ship strong female character number one back to Coruscant to answer for her crimes. Now, you might think it would make sense to just take her back on their own ship because why wouldn't you do that, right? You're there with her already and there's two of you. So, you could keep an eye on her and make sure that she doesn't try to escape. Because if she really is responsible for killing a Jedi despite all the evidence pointing

against it, then clearly she's smart, resourceful, and powerful. You don't want to take any chances with this one, lads. Or you could put her on a regular old prison transport with minimal security and just assume that everything's going to work out fine. And wouldn't you know it, within about 2 minutes, the other convicts stage a prison break, overpower the one and only guard droid on board, and leave her alone to crash land on the planet, which she survives because an uncontrolled

crash from orbital velocity is definitely the sort of thing you can walk away from. Anyway, back on Coruscant, we're introduced to Squid Game guy, who receives word from Strong Female character number three that Strong Female character number one's ship has crashed. They know this because they picked up the escape prisoners from the escape pod, but they haven't gotten around to investigating the crash site yet and are thinking about sending out probe droids to take a quick look.

Okay, back up a second because I really want to go over this one. If you recovered these prisoners from the escape pod and brought them all the way back to Coruscant, that must mean that you already had at least one Republic ship in orbit around the planet to recover the escape pod in the first place. So, you're telling me that instead of investigating the crash site for survivors, which would have been on the planet directly below, you instead brought the prisoners all the way back

to Republic headquarters and then asked for instructions. So, like, are you [ __ ] or what? Also, if strong female character number three looks familiar to you, you might recognize her as showrunner Leslie Hedland's wife. I'm sure that had absolutely nothing to do with her casting in this show, just like James Gun doesn't let family connections influence his casting choices either. Anyway, Squid Game guy decides that he wants to take a look at the site for himself because strong female character

number one was his padawan once upon a time. So he rounds up dumb male character and strong female character number two and heads off to the crash site to investigate and almost immediately runs into her after ripping off a scene from the fugitive. Then he explains that strong female antagonist is definitely dead because he saw her die in a fire when she was a kid. So strong female character number one is the only person who could possibly have committed this crime. But then strong

female character number one is like I didn't do it. Trust me, bro. and he's like, "Well, I'm convinced. [ __ ] me. You're easy to win over, aren't you?" It's good to see that a Jedi Knight won't listen to evidence, logic, or reason. But a single unverified claim by a woman with absolutely no reason to be truthful is enough to totally change his perspective. Like, I'm very much in the camp that rational evidence would be enough to exonerate her in this case.

But if you're not even willing to listen to that and are still convinced that her twin sister died because you apparently saw it happen, then what other conclusion can you draw here? You know, here's a crazy idea. How about you do that forced mind readading thing that you did earlier with one of the escape prisoners? That would clear up everything pretty quickly. Either she would open her mind to you and you'd be able to tell if she was innocent or guilty, or if she tried to block you, it

would be a pretty strong indication that she was trying to cover up her own guilt. But nah, one quick conversation's enough to change his mind, I guess. Meanwhile, strong female antagonist goes for a walk on a beach and meets up with Discount Venom, who delivers what may be one of the most nonsensical monologues in the entire show. >> Steel or laser are no threat to them. >> She literally did kill a Jedi with a [ __ ] blade less than 30 minutes ago. Like, do these people even read their

own scripts? Do they even think about what they're writing? Then he ignites a red lightsaber for no other reason than because they want you to know that he's a Sith. And that's it. That's episode one over.

What a completely [ __ ] mess this show is. It's built around literally the simplest by the numbers murder mystery plot complete with twin sister fem fatal tropes and everything and somehow still finds a way to [ __ ] it up. And it's all held together by insane coincidences, ridiculous contrivances, and decisions that run contrary to logic and common sense. Andor showed us how a murder investigation can be conducted in a smart, gripping, tension-building way

with a combination of deductive logic, interesting character moments, and explosive bursts of gritty action. You get the feeling they were trying real hard to emulate that tone and feel in this show. The only things they're missing are brains, creativity, imagination, and writing skill. And that's not the only problems at play here, either. The acting is terrible. The fight choreography is weak. Nobody looks like they actually want to be here. And the pacing is god awful. The

episode could probably have been wrapped up in under 20 minutes without all the pointless timewasting travel backwards and forwards and redundant conversations telling us stuff we just saw happen. We're only one episode in so far, and I can already see how they're having to stretch the paper thin plot to try to spin it out into eight episodes. This show might aspire to be something like Andor, but it's more like Book of Boba Fett. And believe me, it only gets worse

with episode 2, but I'm going to have to cover that in a separate video because I don't think my mind or my liver can handle too much of this stuff all at once. You know, reviewing the cinematic calamity that is the acolyte is a bit like being one of those poor guys shoveling radioactive debris off the roof at Chernobyl. You have to limit your exposure time. Otherwise, it's a one-way trip to Moscow for you, comrades. Anyway, since I've taken it upon myself to document the full scale

of this disaster piece for as long as my brain and liver can hold up, it's onwards and downwards with episode two. 30 Minutes of TV, which I'm relatively convinced was written by a group of chimpanzees with learning difficulties. The episode opens with strong female antagonists sneaking into a Jedi temple on generic planet number 4271. This is where she encounters token white guy who's floating in the middle of the room meditating or something. And let's just

take a moment to appreciate this guy's character design, shall we? I didn't think it was possible to simultaneously look like an old man and a small child, but apparently they found a way to make it happen. We also learn later in the episode that Token White Guy's been here for more than 10 years and hasn't spoken a word to anyone that whole time. So, like, has he just been floating here in the middle of the room for a decade? How does he eat or sleep or take a force

dump? Are Jedi allowed to just abandon their responsibilities and go in the huff for 10 years with no repercussions or official punishment? Who knows? The showrunners almost certainly don't because I'm willing to bet their thinking on this one didn't extend much beyond, wouldn't it be cool if strong female antagonist has to kill a Jedi, but he's protected by a magical [ __ ] force field, so she has to find another way to kill him instead. So anyway, strong female antagonist does her

patented come at me bro routine. >> Attack me with all your strength >> and tries to kill token white guy, but he's protected by a magical [ __ ] force field and all her attacks just kind of bounce off him. I love how the force has gone from this subtle mystical power that gives people some limited influence on the physical world to just full-on magic that can do whatever the script needs it to. Truly, George Lucas must be proud of what they've done with his creation. Now, you might think that

strong female antagonist would have been prepared for a contingency like this. I mean, this force field might be able to deflect knives and fists, but I'm pretty sure it's not impenetrable. Maybe she brought along poison gas that could seep through the force field and choke him to death, or an explosive charge that would collapse the temple on top of his heads, or a thermal detonator that would vaporize everything in the vicinity. I mean, they might not be the most elegant

solutions, but if you've only got one chance to kill this guy, then you'd be wise to take any opportunity that you can. But nah, instead she's just like, "Well, I'm out of ideas." and runs away. This is where we hook up with strong female character number one and Squid Game guy, who get word of the assassination attempt from strong female character number three and set out to investigate. Meanwhile, strong female antagonist meets up with discount Ezra Miller, which I realize is a misleading

name since it implies that Ezra Miller has any intrinsic value to begin with, but hey, at least he got there first. Anyway, Discount Ezra Miller's killed the owner of a local drugstore and taken over the place. Why? Don't know. But if your intention here is to operate covertly, then killing a local businessman and stealing his shop probably isn't the best way to go about it. I mean, people generally come to know local shop owners because they're an important part of the community. And

if one of them was to go missing suddenly, then I'm pretty sure they'd start asking questions. And the first person they'd ask is the random [ __ ] that's suddenly squatting in his shop. Anyway, those are questions for a different show. A show written by functioning adults who actually understand cause and effect, and that's not what The Acolyte is about. So anyway, discount Ezra Miller gives strong female antagonist a poison to kill token white guy since weapons

apparently don't work on him. Now at first I have to admit that I thought this was a good idea. Maybe it's some kind of airborne pathogen that his force field can't protect him from or acid strong enough to melt his fake beard off. But nah it's just actual poison that he has to drink. >> Well that's definitely shite. >> I don't think you really appreciate the dimensions of this particular problem. discount Ezra Miller. If she can't physically reach him, then she can't

force him to drink the poison. And so, the mission's a bust. So, if we're assuming that he knew she was going to need that poison, which is why he took over the drugstore, then why the hell didn't he give this to her on her first attempt at killing this guy? Why did he wait for her to fail and then come back for more help? Isn't that just putting a massive target on your back? But that's okay, because I guess strong female antagonist has read the script already

and knows that everything's going to work out fine. Now, you might think that if there'd been an assassination attempt on the local Jedi by someone who's already murdered at least one Jedi master, it would make sense to beef up security around the temple, lock the place down, have armed guards stationed at every entry point, maybe even move token white guy to a more secure location. So, it was refreshing to see that they did absolutely [ __ ] all in this case, and the only thing that was

beefed up around here was this body positive individual. You know, I seem to recall reading that Jedi practice intense mental and physical discipline, training their bodies and minds to the very peak of their capabilities to give themsel the best possible chance in the event of armed conflicts. The thing is, mental and physical discipline are not words that come to mind when I look at the big lad here. No wonder token white guy lives in a force bubble for 10

years. The only thing I'd trust this guy to defend me from is an all you can eat buffet. So Squid Game guy and strong female character arrive at the temple, but they're too late to stop strong female antagonist who slips inside completely unopposed because everyone in this episode is [ __ ] and gives token white guy the poison. And he's like, "Well, guess it's time to die then." And promptly murders himself. Oh man. Oh god. Oh man. I genuinely don't understand what just took place

here. Like if token white guy places so little value on his own life, then why did he even resist strong female antagonist earlier? Why not just let her kill you? Jesus, even Kerianne Moss got to put up some kind of fight. But this chump just agrees to die without even a whimper. >> Oh yes, I'll shake. >> [ __ ] off, token white guy, and take your stupid 1970s hairdo with you. Anyway, with their mission complete, you'd think it would make sense for strong female

antagonist and discount Ezra Miller to get off the planet as quickly as possible. After all, they've done what they need to do here, and the Jedi are clearly on to them now, so there's no reason to stay here a second longer. But because everyone in this episode is [ __ ] they decide to hang around in the vicinity of the crime scene for absolutely no reason, which gives Squid Game guy and strong female character a chance to catch up to them by tracking the poison back to the only drugstore in

the area, which discount Ezra Miller is conveniently still living in so that the rest of the plot can happen. [ __ ] You'd think at this point they would start to investigate what common factor links the two Jedi victims, like a past mission that they went on together, for example. I'm no expert, but I'm pretty sure that's basic police procedure when dealing with serial killers like this. Trying to understand what connects the victims. Anyway, this is when something happens which is so

monumentally dumb, it makes everything else in this episode look like a Christopher Nolan movie by comparison. Strong female character decides that it's best to go into the shop posing as strong female antagonist so that she can get a confession from discount Ezra Miller about what happens. Why can't you just go in there, arrest him, and use that forced mindreading trick from the first episode to rip the information you need right out of his heads? Why are you wasting time with this ridiculous

contrived [ __ ] Why doesn't discount Ezra Miller immediately realize that something's up when his friend starts acting like a completely different person and asking probing questions about stuff that they both already know? Why does nothing in this show make any sense? The thing is, this is exactly what dumb male character suggested and it was played off as some ridiculous plan and shot down immediately by strong female character number two when in reality it was a much more logical and

reliable way of getting the information they needs. Anyway, so the gang spring their idiotic trap and Squid Game guy gets into a martial arts battle with strong female antagonist because that's apparently how fighting gets done in Star Wars now. And then she escapes by knocking some dust in their faces. You in full, [ __ ] man. >> Yeah, sure. Let's go with that. And strong female character tries to stop her with a stun gun, but misses for some reason. Like, she's really conflicted by

this whole thing. It's a stun gun, you [ __ ] [ __ ] It's designed to stun people without harming them. You're not being asked to kill her. There's no moral dilemma to any of this. Just stun her, apprehend her, and then pick up the pieces later. But instead, she lets her go so that the rest of the plot can happen. son. >> Then strong female antagonist links back up with discount Ezra Miller and decides to trust him again despite the fact that he told the Jedi their entire plan and

has definitely compromised their mission beyond any hope of completion because everyone in this episode is [ __ ] You know, they say that characters are only as smart as the people writing them, and I think the acolyte is a perfect demonstration of that. Nobody in this show acts with anything, even approaching common sense or logic. People make completely inongruous decisions that actively work against their own interests for no other reason than to keep this absurd parody of a

story trundling forwards. The problem with a murder mystery plot is that you actually have to build up and maintain a mystery to keep the audience invested. And it's pretty difficult to do that when every character spills their guts about everything that's happening at the very first opportunity and all the major players are so completely boring and unlikable that you don't even care who lives or dies anyway. Everything about this episode is idiotic, childly simplistic, and so badly implemented

that it's not just hamfisted, it's sausage fingered and bacon wristeds. It's a textbook example of people hired for their identity rather than their talent, reaching for something that's far beyond their intellectual grasp and blowing hundreds of millions of dollars in the process. The Acolyte is a show made by idiots intended for morons, which even the most diehard Star Wars fanboys would struggle to defend at this point. The power of blood. >> Yes. Yes. The power of two. [Applause]
>> The power of many.

[Applause] >> Yes. Yes. Episode 3 of The Acolyte was without a doubt one of the most devastatingly embarrassing spectacles I think I've ever witnessed on TV. 40 solid minutes of relentless, unadulterated, unapologetic, unaware cringe distilled down to its purest and most toxic form. A show made by people who seem to despise not just Star Wars, but the very concept of film making and storytelling in general. It's quite literally anti-enter entertainment made by anti-

geniuses and I do believe it may have broken what's left of the Star Wars fandom forever. Anyway, there's an extra-large bottle of toilet duck with my name on it. So, let's get this over with as quickly as possible. The episode begins 16 years before the main events of the show on the peaceful planet of whatever stupid name the writers concocted this time. And we get to see strong female character and strong female antagonist when they were still little kids. I know this because of the

subtle visual cues like them still having the exact same hairstyle they'll have 16 years later. Because as we all know, everyone chooses to stay exactly the same forever. Anyway, strong female character and strong female antagonist live with their two mums in a coven of communist lesbian space witches that have chosen to isolate themselves from the rest of the Republic because the galaxy doesn't tolerate women like them. Oh my goodness, I wonder what that could

possibly be a reference to. The writing is so subtle on this show. You see, I just can't make out what they're driving at. Also, this is just a crazy thought on my part, but if you really want to remain hidden from the rest of the galaxy, maybe don't build your communist lesbian space witch community in a gigantic fortress on top of a mountain that's so big it can probably get seen from space. Are you dumb or something? >> Anyway, this is where the show tries its

hand at character development. And I use that word about as loosely as I'd use the term fine dining to describe a pot noodle. The two strong female twins are due to go through a big ceremony to turn them into full-on witches because they were created without a father using the power of the force. Sorry, the thread. But don't worry, we'll get into that soon enough. Believe that. But strong female character dreams of leaving the communist lesbian space witches behind and seeing what else is

out there in the galaxy. Strong female antagonist, on the other hand, wants to stay right here and refuses to let her twin leave because she's a psychotic lunatic. Hm. I wonder if this is going to have repercussions later. So, it's time for us to meet their moms, strong female character number four and strong female character number five, each of whom have slightly different perspectives on how to raise their strong female twins. Strong female character number five believes in

discipline, caution, and suspicion of outsiders, while strong female character number four is more indulgent and freethinking. And apparently, she gets the last word on how they're raised because she created them with the power of the thread. >> I carried them. I created them. >> Liar, lie, your pants on fire. Now, you might have thought that Anakin was the only example of immaculate conception in Star Wars, but you'd be wrong, you bigot. Because it turns out that not one

but two strong female characters got there like a hundred years earlier. Why do I get timeless children and Star Trek Discovery vibes from all of this? Also, we might as well get this thread [ __ ] out of the way. So, like you know how the force has been one of the defining elements of Star Wars for almost 50 years, referenced in every single movie, game, and TV show ever made? Well, it turns out we were all wrong. Because according to the lesbian communist space witches, the real nature

of it is a thread that binds all living things together. It's not a power that can be controlled or used as a weapon like those mean stinky Jedi do. Also, it's funny because literally seconds later, strong female character number four actually does use the thread as a weapon. How embarrassing. And the more I think about that, the more it seems like a perfect metaphor for people just like this, claiming one thing and then doing the exact opposite without ever acknowledging their own

hypocrisy. Jesus, imagine still being a Star Wars fan at this point and having to reconcile all these conflicting [ __ ] ideas in your heads. >> Yes. Yes. Anyway, it's time for one of the most cringe-inducing scenes in all of Star Wars. And that's really saying something at this point. >> Power to the power.

>> When I saw this, I actually had to pause the playback, walk away, and really just let it sink in. This is a thing that actually got made. real functioning adults wrote, performed, edited, and scored this. And at no point in the process did anyone raise their hands and say, "I think this might be a bad idea, guys." This is exactly what happens when you take people who consider this to be thought-provoking art and put them in charge of a $200 million Star Wars show. Truly, George Lucas must

be so proud of what they've accomplished with his baby. Anyway, luckily for us, the ceremony gets interrupted when the mean, stinky Jedi show up to kidnap the strong female twins because they think they could be powerful in the force or something. You know, it's funny how there seems to be this real concerted effort by Disney to paint the Jedi in the worst possible light so that they can be replaced with something more modern. Anyway, strong female character

number five doesn't appreciate this much and uses the thread as a weapon even though it totally isn't a weapon according to them to threaten token white guy with annihilation. Because really, who else out of this extremely diverse group of Jedi could she possibly choose as her victim? Anyway, eventually strong female Jedi is able to calm things down and Squid Guy tries to win strong female character over to his side by handing her a lightsaber to play with.

>> Are you dumb or something? Are you out of your [ __ ] mind? She's a small child and this is one of the deadliest weapons in the galaxy. Is this really how you want to attract someone to your cause? Like, what if she accidentally ignites it and cuts her own head off? [ __ ] mission failed right there, you dick. Anyway, so the Jedi's plan is to test the strong female twins to see if they've got force potential. So, the communist lesbian space witches hatch the genius plan for them to

intentionally fail the test so they'll be left alone. >> Yes. Yes. Wow, four-dimensional quantum mega chest going on there, ladies. Well done. Unfortunately, it all goes wrong when strong female character gets found out in a matter of seconds and decides to just pass the test anyway. So, strong female antagonist does the smart thing and locks her in her room and then sets her journal on fire in the middle of a stone hallway in a fortress that's made predominantly of stone. I mentioned the

fact that it's made of stone so often because the fire quickly spreads and then engulfs the entire fortress because apparently in this universe stone is more combustible than a 1990s shell suit. Oh yeah, and the communist lesbian space witches all die from boredom or something. Then strong female antagonist falls into a pit and strong female character gets rescued by Squid Game guy who agrees to take her to Coruscant and train her as a Jedi. But wouldn't you know it, strong female antagonist

actually survived and begins her revenge trip. Well, who could have predicted that when we totally know that she's alive 16 years later? You know, reviewing this episode is kind of an exercise in futility at this point because really, what is the point anymore in complaining about nonsensical and contradictory writing, the horrendous cinematography, the wooden acting, and the terrible dialogue? Those things are all part for the course in the Acolyte. You almost expect it now.

The bigger problem now is that it's honestly difficult to feel much of anything for the acolyte or Star Wars in general. Because I think this show is proof, if you ever needed it, that this is just how things are going to be now. Star Wars isn't going to be saved by anyone. It's not going to go back to the way it was, and it's not going to improve in any meaningful way because there's no one left with the will or the talent to do it. The Acolyte represents their ultimate vision for this

franchise. It's the perfect distillation of everything Disney and the so-called creatives at Lucasfilm want to inject into George Lucas's vision, and they're not going to stop. Kathleen Kennedy recently bemoaned all the criticism that she and women like Leslie Hedland have taken for their creative decisions because the predominantly male fan base just can't seem to recognize their genius. Well, congratulations, Kathy, because that seems to be a problem that you've gradually solved with every new

show and movie that you've produced. If there's no fans left at all, then there's no toxic male fans left either. Genius. So, in that respect, at least, I guess you've kind of won by default. Congratulations. Star Wars is all yours now. And this here is your prize. An empire of dust, a franchise in ruins. And really, I hope it was all worth it. I hope it was worth the billions of dollars you've blown on failed projects over the years. I hope it was worth provoking the contempt and finally the

apathy of millions of Star Wars fans that have just chosen to walk away. I hope it was worth it to have a legacy of absolute failure. The Sith have been extinct for a millennium. >> I have no name, but the Jedi like you might call me Sith. Sith Sith. >> What? If you attack a Jedi with a weapon, you will fail. Steel or laser are no threat to them. >> Are you serious? >> Just when I thought I was out, they pull me back in. >> You know, after the absolute dumpster

fire that was episode 3, I genuinely thought that I was done with the acolyte. I mean, episode 4 was basically 30 minutes of people walking aimlessly through really fake looking jungle sound stages to the point where I couldn't even think of anything interesting to say about it. But throughout the promotion for this show, Leslie Heddlin kept threatening us with a lightsaber battle that was going to rival the prequels in terms of spectacle and awesomeness. And I guess this is what

she must have had in mind because as much as the previous episode was defined by its complete lack of action, this one is entirely consumed by it. I'm not kidding. A good 80% of this episode is people dueling, punching each other, kicking each other, force pushing, force levitating, and headbutting lightsabers. But don't worry, we'll get into that one soon enough. Believe that. First up though, if I had to be super generous towards this show, because never let it

be said that I don't find the positives in even the most depressingly terrible examples of modern entertainment, I'll at least say that there's a little bit of skill and stakes involved in this one. I mean, it's still let down by horrifically bad writing, of course. Character motivations flip on a dime, and people constantly make nonsensical decisions that actively work against their own interests. But at least the fight choreographers earned their paychecks this time around, unlike other

shows I could mention. Anyway, allow me to bring you up to speed on current events. This won't take long. So, in episode 4, strong female antagonist went to Jungle Planet to kill Jedi Wookie, only to have a change of heart for absolutely no reason and surrender herself to Squid Game Guy and the others. But then, Smileo Ren showed up at the end to totally crash the party. And that's it. That's literally everything that happened in that episode. Why do I get the feeling that

this show was originally going to be like four episodes long, but they were told to spin it out into eight to make it seem more impressive? Anyway, whatever. Episode 5 picks up with the resulting battle of Squid Game guy, dumb male character, strong female character number two, and a bunch of nameless extras versus Smileo Ren. And it carries on with that premise for the next 21 minutes. I know this because I timed it while watching because I had nothing better to do, frankly. And this brings

me to my first major issue with the episodes. You know how one of the big complaints about the final duel in Revenge of the Sith is that for all its visual spectacle, it goes on for so long that it kind of burns out its momentum and exhausts the audience. Well, that one lasted for a grand total of 11 minutes and 42 seconds of screen time, which is roughly half the length of the battle in this episodes. And that's not even counting all the additional fights

that happen afterwards, or the fact that this one takes place on one jungle location at night, which is neither interesting nor visually engaging. As I keep reminding Tatiana, just because it's longer doesn't make it better. There's also no consistent motivation for any of the characters involved in it. Dom male character, for example, is ordered to escort strong female protagonist back to the ship to the point where he's willing to physically restrain her if she doesn't obey him

until she tells him that they have to go back because Smileo Ren wants to kill their friends. And he's like, "Okay, did you not realize that he was there to kill everyone right off the bat? Why do you think he showed up with a lightsaber, you dick?" thing I have to ask is, what the [ __ ] was the point in any of this, what was the show even trying to accomplish? What was it trying to say to us as an audience? I honestly felt like I had no idea who the main character was even supposed to be, who the villains were, what anyone was trying to achieve, or whether I was supposed to empathize with them or despise them because every single aspect of the writing is so god-awful and ass backwards that there's no reason to care about any of it. Osha

goes from being Smile Ren's prisoner one minute to leading him directly to May the next, even though he's out to kill May, and I don't even know what the [ __ ] Osha plans to do with her. May runs away from Squid Game Guy even though she wanted to kill him in the previous episode and knows that he's going to follow her anyway. So, what does she expect to achieve there? And Squid Game guy ran away from the Jedi in the last episode, but then activates a tracker to

lead them straight to him in this one. Why? The finale for any show is supposed to bring together all the various character arcs, plot threads, and mysteries to deliver a satisfying conclusion for all of it. But all we get here is a bunch of morally confused characters we don't particularly know or care about, with motivations that change from one scene to the next, all converging on the same place for unknown reasons. And setting aside all the [ __ ] identity politics that are

basically just par for the course now with Disney products, the moral framework of this show is completely and utterly [ __ ] Characters that are supposed to be unmasked as villains have done absolutely nothing to deserve it, while others commit the most despicable acts possible and then get completely forgiven in a matter of minutes because moral relativism. Morality does not function like that. You [ __ ] spam sandwiches. You can't go around killing innocent people who

are just doing their jobs and expect the audience to empathize with you later. Whatever you care to define storytelling as. The acolyte is the exact opposite of it. Nobody who had even a hand in writing this should be allowed within a nautical mile of a writer's room for the rest of their natural lives. It's not just laughably bad from an acting, production, and directing point of view. But when you really think about it, it's downright shocking that something like

this was even commissioned in the first place. Any studio head with an ounce of common sense would have taken one look at this script and deposited it in the nearest landfill. But I guess things work very differently at Lucasfilm. Instead, this is what we got. A $180 million joke that's done nothing except further destroy an already crippled brand and unite the entire internet in absolute mockery and disgusts. Just another [ __ ] Star Wars project to add to the ever growing list, destined to be
forgotten within days of it being over. And as far as I'm concerned, good riddens. See you in hell, Akoshite. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
